The bay itself is beautiful, with a curved beach and clear water popular for swimming in summer. The small settlement retains a community spirit with a pub, general store, and backpacker accommodation. Tokomaru Bay is on the Pacific Coast Highway (SH35) around the East Cape, about two hours north of Gisborne.

reviews.summary: A hauntingly beautiful heritage site with a powerful story of boom and decline. The ruins are photogenic and the bay is lovely. A highlight of the East Cape road trip.

pois.name: East Cape Road Trip (SH35)
pois.short: Scenic coastal highway around the East Cape, one of NZ's most remote and culturally rich road journeys.
pois.commentary: State Highway 35 around the East Cape is one of New Zealand's most rewarding road trips, tracing 334 kilometres of rugged Pacific coastline between Opotiki and Gisborne. This is one of the least-travelled main highways in the country, passing through predominantly Maori communities with beautifully carved meeting houses, small coastal settlements, and pristine beaches. Highlights include Te Araroa, home to New Zealand's oldest pohutukawa tree; the East Cape Lighthouse, the first place in mainland New Zealand to see the sunrise; the ornate Tikitiki Church with its stunning Maori carvings; and the surf breaks of Hicks Bay and Tokomaru Bay. Allow at least two days to do the journey justice, with overnight stops available at small motels, campgrounds, and backpackers along the route. Fuel up whenever you can, as petrol stations are infrequent. The road is sealed throughout but narrow and winding in places.

reviews.summary: An authentic, off-the-beaten-track New Zealand experience far from tourist crowds. The coastal scenery is stunning and the Maori cultural presence is deeply felt. Allow more time than planned as the road is slow.

pois.name: Eastwoodhill Arboretum
pois.short: New Zealand's national arboretum with the Southern Hemisphere's largest collection of Northern Hemisphere trees.
pois.commentary: Eastwoodhill Arboretum near Ngatapa, 35 kilometres from Gisborne, is New Zealand's national arboretum and home to the largest collection of Northern Hemisphere trees in the Southern Hemisphere. Founded by Douglas Cook who began planting in 1910, the 131-hectare property contains over 25,000 trees from 4,000 species, including oaks, maples, beeches, and conifers from around the world. The arboretum is spectacular in autumn (April-May) when the deciduous trees create a blaze of colour rarely seen in New Zealand. Walking tracks of varying length wind through different collection areas, from formal avenues to wild garden areas. The Homestead provides accommodation for overnight stays. Spring flowering and summer shade offer year-round appeal, but the autumn display is the primary draw. The arboretum operates on a modest entry fee and is open daily. A remarkable living legacy of one man's vision for conservation and beauty.

reviews.summary: A stunning arboretum with extraordinary autumn colour that rivals any Northern Hemisphere display. The variety of trees is remarkable. A hidden gem that deserves to be better known.

pois.name: Tolaga Bay Wharf
pois.short: The longest concrete wharf in the Southern Hemisphere, stretching 660 metres into Tolaga Bay.
pois.commentary: Tolaga Bay Wharf is the longest concrete wharf in the Southern Hemisphere, stretching an impressive 660 metres into the Pacific Ocean. Built in 1929 to serve the coastal shipping trade, the wharf was used for loading wool, frozen meat, and dairy products until closure in 1969. Today it stands as a heritage landmark and popular walking destination, with the journey to the end rewarded by ocean views and occasional dolphin sightings. The wharf has been strengthened and is safe to walk, though some sections show the weathering of decades of salt and storm exposure. Tolaga Bay itself is a beautiful curved beach with clear water, popular for swimming and surfing. The nearby Cook's Cove walkway leads to the cove where Captain Cook anchored in 1769, passing through a spectacular natural rock arch. Located on SH35 about an hour north of Gisborne, Tolaga Bay is a key stop on the East Cape road trip.

reviews.summary: An impressive heritage structure with a rewarding walk to the end. The ocean views and sense of history are compelling. Combine with the Cook's Cove walk for a full Tolaga Bay visit.

pois.name: Tūpapa Heritage Trail
pois.short: 4km trail with 10 markers from waterfront to Kaiti Hill sharing tangata whenua stories.
pois.commentary: Tūpapa is an app-guided heritage trail weaving 4 kilometres through central Gisborne, from the inner harbour to the summit of Kaiti Hill. Ten interpretation points share the stories of tangata whenua — the people who have lived here for centuries before Cook's arrival.

reviews.summary: Walkers appreciate the depth of storytelling and the app-guided format. The trail gives genuine insight into Māori history that most visitors would otherwise miss.

pois.name: Tatapouri Reef Ecology Experience
pois.short: Walk on reef at low tide to encounter wild stingrays, eagle rays, and marine life.
pois.commentary: At low tide, Tatapouri's reef platform becomes a natural aquarium. Guided by local operators, visitors wade onto the exposed reef to encounter wild short-tailed stingrays, eagle rays, and a rich array of marine invertebrates.

reviews.summary: An unforgettable wildlife encounter — stingrays glide right up to you. Guides are knowledgeable about marine ecology.

pois.name: Cooks Cove Walkway & Hole in the Wall
pois.short: Walk through farmland to secluded coves and dramatic cliff opening.
pois.commentary: From the end of Wharf Road near Tolaga Bay, this track crosses farmland and climbs to a saddle before descending to Cooks Cove — where the Endeavour anchored in October 1769. The star attraction is the Hole in the Wall, a massive natural arch punched through a rocky headland by the sea.

reviews.summary: The Hole in the Wall is spectacular and worth the walk. Farmland section is exposed on hot days. Swimming through the arch on calm days is a highlight.

pois.name: Gisborne Rail Bike Adventure
pois.short: Pedal tandem bikes along abandoned coastal railway line — unique NZ experience.
pois.commentary: The Gisborne Rail Bike Adventure repurposes a section of the disused Gisborne-Napier railway line, letting visitors pedal specially designed tandem rail bikes along the tracks through coastal scenery.

reviews.summary: Unique and fun — nothing else like it in NZ. Coastal scenery is beautiful. Easy enough for families with children.

pois.name: Titirangi / Kaiti Hill Reserve
pois.short: Hilltop with 360° views over Gisborne, Cook Landing Site, and sacred significance.
pois.commentary: Kaiti Hill — known by its Māori name Titirangi — rises above the confluence of the Waimata, Taruheru, and Tūranganui rivers, offering commanding 360-degree views over Gisborne and Poverty Bay.

reviews.summary: Outstanding panoramic views and a place of real historical significance. The drive or walk up is easy. The dual Cook/Te Maro memorials prompt genuine reflection.

pois.name: Wyllie Cottage
pois.short: Oldest European house in Gisborne — colonial domestic life preserved.
pois.commentary: Wyllie Cottage is the oldest surviving European building in Gisborne, dating from the early 1870s. Now preserved in the grounds of Tairāwhiti Museum.

reviews.summary: A small but interesting heritage building, best visited as part of a Tairāwhiti Museum visit.

pois.name: Sunshine Brewery
pois.short: Gisborne's own craft brewery producing distinctive East Coast ales.
pois.commentary: Sunshine Brewery has been crafting beer in Gisborne since 1989, making it one of New Zealand's pioneering craft breweries.

reviews.summary: A genuine craft brewery with character and history. The Gisborne Gold is a standout.

pois.name: Young Nick's Head / Te Kuri-a-Pāoa
pois.short: The first land sighted by Cook's crew in 1769, named after cabin boy Nicholas Young.
pois.commentary: Young Nick's Head — Te Kuri-a-Pāoa in te reo Māori — is the dramatic headland at the southern end of Poverty Bay. It was the first land sighted from the Endeavour on 6 October 1769.

reviews.summary: A historically significant landmark best viewed from the Gisborne waterfront or Kaiti Hill.

pois.name: Motu River Trails
pois.short: Remote wilderness river offering jet boat and white water rafting adventures.
pois.commentary: The Motu River is one of New Zealand's last untouched wilderness rivers, flowing through remote gorges and native bush in the Raukūmara Range.

reviews.summary: True wilderness — feels like you're the first person to see this landscape. Rafting trips are exceptional.

pois.name: Morere Hot Springs
pois.short: Natural hot springs in lush native bush — public and private pools surrounded by nikau forest.
pois.commentary: Morere Hot Springs is a small, DOC-managed hot springs complex set in a beautiful stand of nikau palm forest.

reviews.summary: A hidden gem — natural hot pools in beautiful bush setting. Very relaxing and uncrowded.

pois.name: Tokomaru Bay
pois.short: Atmospheric small East Cape settlement with ruined wharf, surf beach, and strong community spirit.
pois.commentary: Tokomaru Bay is one of the most atmospheric settlements on the East Cape — a small community centred around a beautiful bay with a ruined concrete wharf.

reviews.summary: Authentic and atmospheric — the ruined wharf is hauntingly beautiful. Great surf beach.

pois.name: Anaura Bay Recreation Reserve
pois.short: Pristine bay where Cook first found fresh water — bush walk, beach, and total peace.
pois.commentary: Anaura Bay is where Cook's crew first found fresh water in New Zealand in October 1769. Today it's a DOC recreation reserve with a pristine beach.

reviews.summary: Pristine and peaceful — one of the most beautiful bays on the East Cape.

pois.name: Te Kuri a Paoa / Young Nick's Head Walk
pois.short: Dramatic coastal walk across farmland to the peninsula that changed NZ history.
pois.commentary: This coastal walk crosses private farmland to reach the Te Kuri-a-Pāoa / Young Nick's Head peninsula — the first land sighted by Cook's crew in 1769.

reviews.summary: A walk through history with stunning coastal scenery. The sense of remoteness at the headland is powerful.

pois.name: Cook Landing Site / Te Toka-a-Taiau
pois.short: Where European and Māori worlds first collided in October 1769 — monuments and interpretive panels at the river mouth.
pois.commentary: Te Toka-a-Taiau — the rock of Taiau — marks the spot at the mouth of the Tūranganui River where the Endeavour's boats first came ashore on 9 October 1769. This national historic reserve preserves the physical place where two worlds collided, and the consequences rippled across centuries. Interpretive panels now tell both stories — the Endeavour's voyage and the much older Polynesian arrivals. The 1906 Cook obelisk stands alongside more recent iwi-carved pou that restore the Māori narrative to the landscape. It's a place for quiet reflection, especially at dawn when the first light in the world touches this shore.
sources_to_check: DOC — Cook Landing National Historic Reserve

reviews.summary: A deeply significant place that rewards contemplation. The dual storytelling — European and Māori perspectives — is well handled. Visit at sunrise for the full emotional impact.

pois.name: Gisborne Botanical Gardens
pois.short: Peaceful riverside gardens in the heart of Gisborne — mature trees, native plantings, and gentle walks.
pois.commentary: The Gisborne Botanical Gardens stretch along the banks of the Taruheru River in the centre of town, offering a calm green retreat from the summer heat. Mature exotic trees — oaks, planes, and magnolias — shade wide lawns, while a dedicated native section showcases coastal and lowland species of the Tairāwhiti region. A children's playground, duck pond, and flat riverside paths make it an easy family outing. The gardens connect to walking tracks that follow the river upstream, and in summer the shallow stretches are popular paddling spots for small children.

reviews.summary: A lovely green oasis in the centre of Gisborne. The river setting is beautiful and the native plant section is well curated. Perfect for a gentle stroll or a family picnic.

pois.name: Waikanae Beach & Surf
pois.short: Gisborne's main beach — excellent surf, safe swimming, and the first place in NZ to see the sunrise.
pois.commentary: Waikanae Beach is Gisborne's front yard — a wide, golden sweep of sand running from the harbour entrance north to the river mouth. The surf is reliable without being intimidating, making it popular with learners and longboarders. Lifeguard-patrolled in summer, the beach has safe swimming zones for families. But the real magic is at dawn: Gisborne is the first city in the world to see the sunrise, and Waikanae Beach is where locals and visitors gather to watch it happen. The Waikanae Surf Life Saving Club anchors the social scene, and the beachfront café strip is one of the most relaxed in the country.

reviews.summary: The sunrise alone is worth the early alarm. Great surf for all levels and a genuinely welcoming beach culture. The café strip makes for a perfect post-swim breakfast.

pois.name: Māhia Peninsula
pois.short: A rugged peninsula of stunning beaches, walking tracks, and home to Rocket Lab's launch site.
pois.commentary: Māhia Peninsula juts into the Pacific between Hawke's Bay and Tairāwhiti like a stubborn fist of land that refused to become an island. The beaches are wild and varied — Ōpoutama faces the open ocean with powerful surf, while the sheltered inner harbour beaches are calm enough for toddlers. Walking tracks cross the headland through regenerating bush with views to both coasts. The peninsula is also home to Rocket Lab's Launch Complex 1 on the remote eastern tip, from where Electron rockets have been launching satellites since 2017 — you may spot a launch tower in the distance. The small settlements have a time-stands-still quality that's increasingly rare in New Zealand.

reviews.summary: One of the most underrated coastal destinations in the North Island. The beaches are spectacular and mostly deserted. The Rocket Lab connection adds a surreal modern twist to this ancient landscape.

pois.name: St Mary's Church, Tikitiki
pois.short: A humble exterior hides one of NZ's most exquisitely decorated church interiors — Māori carving and tukutuku panels honouring WWI fallen.
pois.commentary: St Mary's Church at Tikitiki, on the East Cape road between Te Araroa and Ruatōria, is one of those places where you walk through an ordinary doorway and into something extraordinary. Built in 1924 as a memorial to Ngāti Porou soldiers who served in World War I, the interior is covered floor to ceiling with exquisite Māori carving, tukutuku woven panels, and kōwhaiwhai painted scroll patterns. Each panel tells a story; together they form one of the finest collections of Māori decorative art in any building in New Zealand. The church is still an active place of worship for the local community. Visitors are welcome but should enter respectfully and check for any services in progress.

reviews.summary: Breathtaking — the interior is a masterpiece of Māori art and a deeply moving war memorial. The contrast between the simple exterior and the richness inside is unforgettable. Visit with respect and allow time to absorb the detail.

pois.name: Gisborne Street Art & Murals
pois.short: Growing collection of vibrant murals across central Gisborne telling local stories.
pois.commentary: Over the past few years, Gisborne's central streets have become an open-air gallery of large-scale murals. Local and visiting artists have painted everything from Māori legends to marine life to abstract explosions of colour across building walls, laneways, and fences. The murals are concentrated around Grey Street, Gladstone Road, and the laneways off Peel Street. There's no formal trail map yet — half the fun is wandering and discovering. The works tell distinctly Tairāwhiti stories: the sea, the land, the waka, and the people who have shaped this coast. Free, always open, and constantly evolving.

reviews.summary: A delightful surprise — the murals add real character to the town centre. Great for a casual walking exploration. The quality and variety of work is impressive for a small city.

pois.name: Te Araroa — NZ's Largest Pōhutukawa
pois.short: The largest pōhutukawa tree in NZ — over 600 years old with a massive spreading canopy.
pois.commentary: In the grounds of Te Araroa School, at the very tip of East Cape, stands the largest pōhutukawa tree in New Zealand. Te Waha-o-Rerekohu is estimated at over 600 years old, with a trunk that has split into a forest of secondary trunks supporting a canopy that covers roughly 40 metres across. In December and January, when it blooms, the entire tree turns crimson — a sight that has been greeting travellers arriving at the East Cape for centuries. The tree is easily accessed from the school grounds (signposted from SH35). Treat it with care — it's a living taonga.

reviews.summary: Truly awe-inspiring — the sheer scale of this ancient tree is hard to convey in photos. Worth the drive to the East Cape on its own. Visit in December for the spectacular crimson bloom.

pois.name: Wainui Beach
pois.short: Premier surf beach with consistent point breaks — hosts national surfing competitions.
pois.commentary: Wainui Beach, a ten-minute drive east of Gisborne over the Wainui hill, is where the city's serious surfers go. The beach stretches for several kilometres and picks up swell from multiple directions, meaning there's almost always something rideable. The points at the northern and southern ends produce long, clean walls that have hosted multiple national surfing championships. The local surf culture is strong and welcoming — respect the lineup and you'll have a great time. Beyond surfing, the beach is stunning: white sand backed by pohutukawa-clad headlands, with views south along the coast.

reviews.summary: Consistently great waves in a beautiful setting. The local surf community is friendly and the beach never feels crowded. One of the best surf beaches in the North Island.

pois.name: East Cape Mānuka Company
pois.short: Small East Cape producer making therapeutic mānuka oil and skincare from wild East Cape mānuka.
pois.commentary: The East Cape Mānuka Company operates from the remote tip of the East Cape, harvesting wild mānuka from the surrounding hills to produce therapeutic-grade mānuka oil and a range of skincare products. East Cape mānuka oil has exceptionally high levels of triketones — the compounds that give it its antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties — making it distinct from mānuka oils produced elsewhere in New Zealand. The company offers visitors a chance to learn about the harvesting and steam-distillation process, sample the products, and understand why this particular corner of the country produces such potent oil. A genuine local enterprise rooted in the land.

reviews.summary: Fascinating to see how this unique local product is made. The oil is genuinely exceptional and the staff are passionate about their craft. A worthwhile stop on the East Cape road.

pois.name: Tairāwhiti Navigators' Statues
pois.short: Statues honouring both Captain Cook and Polynesian navigator Kiwa — acknowledging both sides of the first contact story.
pois.commentary: Near the waterfront in Gisborne, a collection of public sculptures tells the navigation stories that converge at Tūranganui-a-Kiwa. A statue of Captain James Cook gazes out to sea, but he's no longer alone in the narrative — nearby, figures and carvings honour the Polynesian navigators who reached these shores centuries earlier. The name Tūranganui-a-Kiwa itself — 'the great standing place of Kiwa' — references the explorer Kiwa who captained the Horouta waka to this coast. The dual acknowledgment of both navigation traditions makes this one of the more thoughtful public art installations in New Zealand, a place where history is held in tension rather than simplified.

reviews.summary: A thought-provoking installation that does justice to both sides of the story. Well worth a pause to read the plaques and reflect. The waterfront setting adds to the sense of arrival and departure.

pois.name: Mount Hikurangi Sunrise Experience
pois.short: Climb the sacred mountain of Ngāti Porou for the first sunrise in the world — guided by local Māori.
pois.commentary: Mount Hikurangi (1,752 m) is the sacred mountain of Ngāti Porou and the first point on mainland New Zealand to see the sun each day. The guided sunrise experience, operated by Ngāti Porou, begins with a 4WD journey up the mountain track in the early hours, followed by a walk to the summit to watch the dawn break over the Pacific. The experience includes karakia (prayer), storytelling, and a hot breakfast. Nine carved whakairo by local artist Te Rongo Kirkwood stand near the summit. Bookings essential — access is only with Ngāti Porou guides.

reviews.summary: Described as life-changing by many visitors. The combination of the physical experience, cultural immersion and stunning sunrise creates something truly special. Book well in advance.

pois.name: Tairāwhiti Museum
pois.short: The region's major museum and art gallery, with Māori taonga from Ngāti Porou and Rongowhakaata, maritime history, C Company memorials, and the heritage Wyllie Cottage on site.
pois.commentary: Tairāwhiti Museum at 10 Stout Street is the cultural anchor of the Gisborne region, housing collections that span 1,000 years of Polynesian and European habitation on the East Coast. The Māori collection includes extraordinary taonga from Ngāti Porou, Rongowhakaata, Te Aitanga-a-Māhaki and other East Coast iwi, many of which are not displayed elsewhere. The maritime gallery tells 1,000 years of stories at sea, from the navigational traditions of the ancestor Māia to the colonial shipping era. The 'C Company' memorial wing honours the Māori Battalion soldiers from the region who served in World War II. Wyllie Cottage (1872) — the oldest European house in Gisborne — sits on the museum grounds. Entry by koha.

reviews.summary: Tairāwhiti Museum is one of the best regional museums in New Zealand — the Māori taonga collection is outstanding in its range and cultural depth, and the maritime gallery adds important Pacific context. The C Company memorial is moving. Entry by donation makes this extraordinary value.

pois.name: Puhi Kai Iti / Cook Landing Site National Historic Reserve
pois.short: The site where Captain Cook first stepped ashore in New Zealand on 8 October 1769 — commemorating both the European arrival and the earlier Polynesian navigators who landed here 400 years before.
pois.commentary: Puhi Kai Iti marks the eastern bank of the Tūranganui River, Gisborne, where Cook landed on 8 October 1769 — the first confirmed European contact with New Zealand. The reserve was redesigned in 2019 to give equal prominence to the Māori story: the waka Horouta and Te Ikaroa-a-Rauru brought the ancestors of local iwi to this exact coast around 1350 AD, 400 years before Cook. The Ikaroa sculpture (2019), created under the guidance of local iwi, honours the navigator Māia and the house of learning Puhi-Kai-Iti that stood here. The reserve includes the original 1906 granite obelisk to Cook — the first monument to a European explorer erected in New Zealand — now presented as one part of a dual narrative, not the only one.

reviews.summary: The 2019 redesign of Cook Landing Site transforms it from a colonial monument into a genuinely bicultural heritage experience. The Ikaroa sculpture and dual narrative give Māori ancestors equal standing with Cook. A deeply thoughtful piece of public heritage interpretation that rewards careful reading.

pois.name: Titirangi Reserve (Kaiti Hill)
pois.short: A 129-metre volcanic hill above Gisborne with panoramic Poverty Bay views, the Cook monument, the James Cook Observatory, and four lookout points — the site holds deep significance for local iwi.
pois.commentary: Titirangi (Kaiti Hill) is the dominant natural landmark of Gisborne, rising directly above the city at the northern entrance to Poverty Bay. The hill is tapu to local iwi — its Māori name refers to the sacred canopy of the sky — and its summit has been a place of ceremony and observation for centuries. The reserve today holds the 1906 Cook granite obelisk, the James Cook Observatory (public viewing sessions), a pōhutukawa planted by Princess Diana in 1983, and four lookout platforms with 360-degree views over the city, Poverty Bay, the Raukūmara Range, and on clear days to Mahia Peninsula and Hawke's Bay. The Tūpapa Heritage Trail connects the city centre to the summit.

reviews.summary: The views from Titirangi are among the finest from any accessible urban hill in New Zealand — the sweep of Poverty Bay, the city, and the distant ranges is genuinely spectacular. The combination of Māori cultural significance, Cook history, and natural panorama makes this the essential starting point for any visit to Gisborne.

pois.name: Te Poho-o-Rāwiri Marae
pois.short: One of New Zealand's largest carved meeting houses — the wharenui of Ngāti Oneone hapū, opened in 1930, housing extraordinary Māori carvings and functioning as an active ceremonial marae.
pois.commentary: Te Poho-o-Rāwiri Marae in the Kaiti suburb of Gisborne is the home marae of Ngāti Oneone hapū (of Ngāti Porou iwi) and contains one of the largest and most elaborately carved wharenui (meeting houses) in New Zealand. The meeting house, opened on 11 March 1930, was constructed under the guidance of master carver Hēnare Ōrupe and features a full interior programme of tukutuku panels, painted kowhaiwhai, and carved pou that constitute a visual history of Ngāti Oneone ancestry. Visits to the marae are by prior arrangement with the marae committee; the exterior can be viewed at any time with respect. The site sits in the shadow of Titirangi Reserve, directly associating the marae with the ancestral hill above.

reviews.summary: Te Poho-o-Rāwiri is one of New Zealand's great carved meeting houses — the scale and quality of the carving programme is exceptional. Pre-arranged visits offer an unparalleled window into East Coast Māori aesthetic traditions and whakapapa narratives. The exterior setting beneath Titirangi is powerful.

pois.name: Young Nick's Head / Te Kurī-a-Pāoa Historic Reserve
pois.short: The headland sighted by Nicholas Young from the Endeavour's masthead on 7 October 1769 — the first land seen by Cook's crew in New Zealand — with Māori associations predating European arrival by centuries.
pois.commentary: Te Kurī-a-Pāoa (Young Nick's Head) is a distinctive promontory at the southern entrance to Poverty Bay, first sighted by Nick Young, the youngest crew member of Cook's Endeavour, on 7 October 1769. Cook named it in his honour, recording it as the first New Zealand land his crew saw. The headland's Māori name refers to the ancestor Paoa, who paddled his dog (kurī) to the shore as part of ancestral navigation traditions — encoding in the landscape's name a story of arrival and recognition far older than Cook's. The reserve is managed by DOC and accessed via a walking track; the views back across Poverty Bay to Gisborne are extensive and historically evocative.

reviews.summary: Young Nick's Head is a place of rare historical layering — Māori traditions of arrival and European first-contact narratives converge on the same headland. The coastal walk and Poverty Bay views are beautiful. Visitors with an interest in navigation history, either Polynesian or European, will find this especially rewarding.

pois.name: Eastwoodhill Arboretum (National Arboretum of New Zealand)
pois.short: New Zealand's National Arboretum — 131 hectares of the largest collection of Northern Hemisphere temperate trees in the Southern Hemisphere, 35 km northwest of Gisborne, spectacular in autumn.
pois.commentary: Eastwoodhill was established by William Douglas Cook from 1910 on the challenging hill country of Ngatapa, driven by a personal obsession with bringing the great deciduous trees of the Northern Hemisphere to New Zealand. The collection now holds more than 4,000 species and cultivars of trees and shrubs from across Europe, Asia and North America — the largest such collection in the Southern Hemisphere. In autumn (April–May), the arboretum is among the most spectacular colour displays in New Zealand, drawing visitors who make the 35-km drive from Gisborne specifically for the experience. The estate now covers 131 hectares and is managed by a trust. A café and accommodation are available on site.

reviews.summary: Eastwoodhill is one of the great garden achievements of New Zealand — the scale and quality of the deciduous tree collection is astounding, and the autumn colour is genuinely world-class. Visitors from all over New Zealand make dedicated trips for the April display. Well worth the detour from Gisborne.

pois.name: Rere Rockslide and Rere Falls
pois.short: A 60-metre natural rock waterslide and an adjacent waterfall on the Wharekopae River — a beloved free outdoor experience 50 km northwest of Gisborne.
pois.commentary: The Rere Rockslide is a 60-metre-long smooth rock face polished by the Wharekopae River over millennia, forming a natural water slide at a gradient steep enough to provide genuine speed. Locals bring rubber mats and bodyboards; the slide is fast, cold, and genuinely thrilling. The adjacent Rere Falls (also called Wairere Falls) drop 5 metres across a wide curtain into a pool — beautiful and swimmable. Both sites are free, managed by the Gisborne District Council, and accessed via a short walk from a gravel car park on Rere Falls Road. The surrounding Wharekopae Valley is scenic hill country with regenerating native bush. The drive from Gisborne passes through the Eastwoodhill Arboretum catchment area.

reviews.summary: The Rere Rockslide is exactly the kind of free, exhilarating natural experience that makes New Zealand special — genuinely fast, completely natural, and immediately addictive. The adjacent falls and swimming hole complete a perfect summer afternoon. Bring a rubber mat and thick shorts.

pois.name: Māhia Peninsula (Te Māhia)
pois.short: A remote, sparsely populated peninsula south of Gisborne with dramatic coastal scenery, significant Māori history, some of New Zealand's best surf breaks, and a rocket launch site.
pois.commentary: Te Māhia (the Māhia Peninsula) juts into Hawke's Bay from the Tairāwhiti coastline, forming a distinct landscape of volcanic bluffs, sandy beaches, and farmland remote enough to retain genuine isolation even in summer. The peninsula is ancestral territory of Ngāti Rongoū and Ngāti Kahungunu, with numerous pā sites on its headlands and deep oral traditions associated with the surrounding sea. The Māhia Beach and Opoutama Beach areas on the eastern coast offer superb surf conditions, while the western side is characterised by fishing and diving. Rocket Lab — the New Zealand private space company — operates its orbital launch facility from the northern tip of the peninsula, making it the world's most active commercial launch site outside the USA.

reviews.summary: Māhia is the kind of slow-travel destination that rewards those who linger — the isolation, the surf, the Māori landscape, and the surreal incongruity of rocket launches add up to a distinctive experience. The drive down the peninsula itself is scenic and unhurried.

pois.name: Hikurangi Maunga (Mount Hikurangi Cultural Experience)
pois.short: The highest non-volcanic peak in the North Island and the sacred ancestral mountain of Ngāti Porou — the first place on Earth to see the sunrise each day — visited by guided cultural experience.
pois.commentary: Hikurangi (1,752 m) is New Zealand's most significant ancestral mountain for Ngāti Porou iwi, associated with the demigod Māui who dragged the North Island (Te Ika-a-Māui) from the ocean — tradition holds that the mountain emerged first, before the rest of the island. As the most easterly high point of New Zealand's mainland, Hikurangi receives the world's first sunrise each day, a fact that gave the mountain global significance at the millennium on 1 January 2000 when the sun rose here before anywhere else on Earth. Cultural tours to the summit are operated exclusively by Ngāti Porou guides and must be pre-booked; they include a pōwhiri at the base marae and guided commentary on the mountain's cultural and geological history. Road access to the carpark is possible but the summit requires a 3-4 hour guided walk.

reviews.summary: A truly extraordinary experience — to stand on the mountain that sees the world's first light, guided by people for whom it is the most sacred place in their universe, is one of the great cultural encounters available anywhere in New Zealand. Requires planning and commitment but is incomparable.

pois.name: Tolaga Bay Wharf and Te Araroa Heritage Drive
pois.short: New Zealand's longest wharf (660 m) at Tolaga Bay, a harbour of significance to both Cook's 1769 voyage and to Māori, and the starting point of a scenic heritage drive up the East Coast.
pois.commentary: Tolaga Bay holds the longest wharf in New Zealand — 660 metres of kauri timber extending across the shallow bay, built in 1929 and now restored as a heritage landmark. Cook's Cove, a 30-minute walk south of the wharf, marks where HMS Endeavour reprovisioned with wood and water in October 1769 — one of only two places Cook landed on the East Coast. The Cook's Cove Walkway passes through an extraordinary landscape of limestone formations, coastal scrub and bluffs carved by the Waipāoa River system. The wharf itself was once a lifeline for East Coast farming communities before roads improved; its scale in such a small settlement is dramatic. The surrounding Tolaga Bay community is predominantly Māori (Uawa Ngāti Porou) with rich cultural associations.

reviews.summary: Tolaga Bay Wharf is one of the most unexpected architectural monuments in New Zealand — 660 metres of heritage timber extending across an undeveloped bay is genuinely arresting. Cook's Cove adds historical depth, and the walkway is one of the better short tracks on the East Coast.

pois.name: Gisborne Wine Region (Chardonnay Capital)
pois.short: New Zealand's 'Chardonnay Capital' — a concentrated wine region in the Poverty Bay flats producing award-winning Chardonnay, Gewürztraminer and Pinot Gris at multiple cellar doors.
pois.commentary: Gisborne is New Zealand's most easterly wine region and claims the title of Chardonnay Capital with justification — the Poverty Bay climate and alluvial soils produce richly flavoured Chardonnay that has won international recognition. The region's key wineries cluster within a 30-km radius of the city, including Matawhero (pioneering producer since 1969), First Light (Māori-owned, nationally awarded), Millton Vineyard (biodynamic, internationally celebrated), and several smaller family operations. The region also excels in Gewürztraminer and Pinot Gris — varieties that perform well in the warm, early-harvest conditions. A self-drive wine trail connects the major cellar doors and can be completed in half a day.

reviews.summary: The Gisborne wine region is compact, high-quality, and refreshingly uncrowded compared to Hawke's Bay or Marlborough. The Chardonnay is legitimately world-class, the Gewürztraminer is outstanding, and the cellar door welcome is warm and personal. First Light's Māori ownership story adds cultural dimension.

pois.name: Te Kuri a Paoa / Poverty Bay Estuary Lookout
pois.short: A lookout above the southern entrance to Poverty Bay, overlooking the estuary and the ancestral landscape of Cook's first landing coast — a place of profound dual-cultural significance.
pois.commentary: Tūranganui-a-Kiwa / Poverty Bay is one of the most culturally layered coastal landscapes in New Zealand — a meeting point of Polynesian navigation traditions going back 700 years and the European age of exploration beginning with Cook in 1769. The southern foreshore below Te Kurī-a-Pāoa offers excellent estuary birdwatching (royal spoonbill, variable oystercatcher, pied stilt) in a landscape little changed since Cook sketched it from his ship. The surrounding farmland was the scene of New Zealand's most violent early contact episode: the deaths of 9 Māori and 1 European over three days in October 1769. Cultural interpretation at the DOC access point contextualises the landscape within both Māori traditions and the Cook voyage.

reviews.summary: A place of quiet historical weight that rewards visitors who engage with the cultural interpretation rather than just the view. The estuary birdwatching is excellent, and the sense of standing in a landscape where New Zealand's contact history effectively began is genuinely powerful.

pois.name: Gisborne i-SITE and Tūranganui River Cultural Walk
pois.short: A free self-guided walk along the Tūranganui River through Gisborne city, passing the historic bridges, Te Toka-a-Taiau commemorative site, and the city's key cultural and heritage buildings.
pois.commentary: The Tūranganui River runs through the heart of Gisborne, its lower reaches historically the scene of first contact between Cook's crew and local Māori in 1769. The river walk connects key heritage sites including the site of Te Toka-a-Taiau (a commemorative rock marking where the original meeting stone once stood — the original was blasted away in 1877 during port development), the Peel Street Bridge (1912), the Poverty Bay War Memorial, and the waterfront reserves that frame the city's civic heart. The Tūpapa Heritage Trail overlaps with this walk in several sections, and together they form a comprehensive self-guided heritage circuit of the city.

reviews.summary: A pleasant, free and culturally informative way to understand the layout and history of Gisborne as a city. The river setting is attractive and the heritage buildings add architectural interest. Best done in conjunction with the Tūpapa app for maximum cultural depth.

pois.name: Rongowhakaata Art and Cultural Centre
pois.short: The cultural home of Rongowhakaata iwi — with significant connections to Te Hau-ki-Tūranga, the world's oldest surviving ornate Māori meeting house, now held at Te Papa.
pois.commentary: Rongowhakaata (literally 'shadow of the clouds') is one of the paramount iwi of the Tairāwhiti region, known throughout New Zealand for their extraordinary artistic traditions — particularly carving, tukutuku weaving, and kōwhaiwhai painting. The iwi's most famous artistic achievement is Te Hau-ki-Tūranga, a wharenui completed around 1842 that is now held at Te Papa Tongarewa in Wellington and considered the finest surviving example of classical Māori architecture. Cultural and art experiences operated through the Rongowhakaata cultural centre in Gisborne bring visitors into direct contact with living artistic traditions, demonstrating that the creative energy represented by Te Hau-ki-Tūranga continues in contemporary Rongowhakaata artists.

reviews.summary: Engaging with Rongowhakaata's living artistic traditions gives meaningful context to the famous works held at Te Papa and elsewhere. The cultural centre programmes are thoughtfully designed and often taught by practising artists. One of the most intellectually rewarding cultural experiences available in the Tairāwhiti region.

pois.name: Eastwoodhill Arboretum
pois.short: Southern Hemisphere's largest collection of Northern Hemisphere trees.
pois.commentary: Eastwoodhill Arboretum near Ngatapa holds the most extensive collection of Northern Hemisphere trees and shrubs in the Southern Hemisphere, planted by visionary Douglas Cook from 1910 until his death in 1984. The 70-hectare grounds contain over 3,500 species of exotic trees including spectacular autumn-colour maples, oaks, and beeches that blaze red, gold, and amber from April to June — the best autumn colour display in New Zealand. Marked walking tracks wind through the collections, ranging from 20-minute strolls to 2-hour circuits. Spring brings flowering cherries, magnolias, and rhododendrons.

reviews.summary: Autumn here is absolutely magical — the best fall colours in NZ. Spring is also stunning. A labour of love that spans a century.

pois.name: Tolaga Bay Wharf
pois.short: New Zealand's longest concrete wharf stretching 660m into the Pacific.
pois.commentary: Tolaga Bay Wharf is the longest concrete wharf in the Southern Hemisphere, stretching 660 metres out into the Pacific Ocean. Built in 1929 to handle coastal shipping for the isolated East Cape community, the wharf fell into disuse when road transport improved but was saved from demolition by local campaigners and restored in the 2000s. Walking its full length is a meditative experience — the shore gradually recedes behind you and you're surrounded by ocean on all sides. Fishers cast lines from the end, and dolphins are regularly spotted from the platform.

reviews.summary: The sheer length of the wharf is remarkable. A peaceful, contemplative walk with ocean views. Dolphins are a frequent bonus.

pois.name: Pacific Coast Highway / SH35
pois.short: 328km coastal road around the East Cape — one of NZ's great undiscovered drives.
pois.commentary: State Highway 35, the Pacific Coast Highway, sweeps 328 kilometres around the East Cape from Opotiki to Gisborne through some of New Zealand's most remote and beautiful coastal scenery. The route passes through predominantly Maori communities, past ancient meeting houses, along deserted surf beaches, and through dense native bush. Highlights include the Raukokore Church standing alone on the beach, the Motu River mouth, Te Araroa and its 600-year-old pohutukawa tree, the East Cape Lighthouse, and Tokomaru Bay's abandoned freezing works. The drive takes at least four hours non-stop but deserves a full day or more with stops.

reviews.summary: One of NZ's most underrated road trips. The remoteness is part of the appeal. Raukokore Church is an unforgettable sight.

pois.name: St Mary's Church Tikitiki
pois.short: Remarkable church blending Christian architecture with intricate Maori carvings and tukutuku panels.
pois.commentary: St Mary's Church at Tikitiki is one of New Zealand's most extraordinary buildings — a small Anglican church whose interior is entirely adorned with intricate Maori carvings, tukutuku panels, and kowhaiwhai painted rafters created by Ngati Porou artisans. Built in 1924 as a memorial to Ngati Porou soldiers who fell in World War I, it represents a remarkable fusion of European church architecture with te ao Maori artistic traditions. Every surface tells a story, from the carved pou depicting ancestors to the woven panels representing local narratives. The church remains an active place of worship. Visitors are welcome but should treat the space with respect.

reviews.summary: A breathtaking fusion of Maori and European art. The interior detail is overwhelming in the best way. A sacred and deeply moving space.

pois.name: Dive Tatapouri — Reef Ecology Experience
pois.short: Walk onto a shallow reef to hand-feed wild stingrays and encounter kingfish.
pois.commentary: Dive Tatapouri offers one of New Zealand's most extraordinary wildlife encounters — walking out onto a shallow coastal reef at low tide to meet wild stingrays and kingfish that voluntarily come in to feed. Named residents like 'Pancake' and 'Waffle' glide around participants' legs while guides share knowledge about the reef ecology. The experience runs at low tide and doesn't require swimming — you simply wade on the reef in provided wetsuits. The stingrays are completely wild and free to come and go, making each encounter genuinely unpredictable. For those wanting more, snorkelling and diving options explore the wider reef.

reviews.summary: Absolutely magical wildlife encounter. The stingrays are gentle and curious. Guides are passionate and knowledgeable.

pois.name: Gisborne Rail Bike Adventure
pois.short: Pedal tandem rail bikes along an abandoned coastal railway through stunning East Coast scenery.
pois.commentary: The Gisborne Rail Bike Adventure is a truly unique experience — pedalling tandem railbikes along a section of abandoned coastal railway line, far from the beaten track. The specially designed bikes attach to the railway tracks, and riders pedal along the coast through a landscape of farmland, bush, and ocean views. The route crosses bridges, passes through cuttings, and follows the coast with views that are impossible to access any other way. It's a relaxed, fun activity suitable for all fitness levels, and the guides share stories of the railway's history and the local area.

reviews.summary: Genuinely unique experience you can't do anywhere else. The coastal scenery is gorgeous. Fun for all ages and fitness levels.

pois.name: Rongopai Painted Meeting House
pois.short: Rare 1880s painted meeting house with unique figurative murals blending Māori and European styles.
pois.commentary: Rongopai is one of the most significant painted meeting houses in New Zealand — built in the 1880s near Waituhi, it features extraordinary figurative murals that blend Māori and European artistic styles in a way that was unique for its time. The paintings depict Māori leaders, biblical figures, and European settlers in a style that departed radically from traditional Māori carving. The house was a political statement by Te Kooti's Ringatū movement. Rongopai is a living marae and visits must be arranged through the local community — this is not a museum but an active cultural space. The painted panels are a national treasure.

reviews.summary: Extraordinary painted meeting house unlike anything else in NZ. A profound cultural experience. Always arrange visits respectfully.

pois.name: St Mary's Church Tikitiki
pois.short: Stunning Māori-decorated WWI memorial church on the East Cape — a fusion of traditions.
pois.commentary: St Mary's Church in Tikitiki is one of New Zealand's most remarkable buildings — a World War I memorial church whose interior is entirely adorned with traditional Māori carvings, tukutuku panels, and painted designs. Built in 1924 to honour Ngāti Porou soldiers who served in WWI, the church fuses Anglican church architecture with Māori artistic traditions in a way that is both culturally significant and visually stunning. Every surface inside is decorated, creating an immersive experience of colour and pattern. The church is a working parish church, not a museum, and visitors should be respectful. It stands as a powerful statement of Ngāti Porou identity and sacrifice.

reviews.summary: One of NZ's most remarkable buildings. The interior decoration is breathtaking. A profound cultural and memorial experience.

pois.name: East Cape Lighthouse — First Sunrise in NZ
pois.short: Climb 700+ steps to mainland NZ's easternmost lighthouse for the first sunrise on Earth (seasonally).
pois.commentary: The East Cape Lighthouse stands at the easternmost point of mainland New Zealand, making it one of the first places on Earth to see the sunrise each day (the exact first-sunrise point shifts seasonally between the lighthouse and Hikurangi). Reaching the lighthouse requires a climb of over 700 steps from the car park — steep but rewarding, especially in the pre-dawn darkness. The sunrise, when it comes, is spectacular — the Pacific Ocean stretching endlessly eastward with nothing between you and South America. The remote setting on the wild East Cape coast, far from any town, adds to the sense of being at the edge of the world. The drive along the East Cape road to reach the lighthouse is scenic.

reviews.summary: Powerful sense of being at the world's edge. The sunrise is spectacular. The 700+ steps are demanding but worth every one.

pois.name: Sunshine Brewery
pois.short: Gisborne's own craft brewery producing beers inspired by the East Coast lifestyle.
pois.commentary: Sunshine Brewery is Gisborne's craft brewery, producing a range of beers that reflect the East Coast's laid-back character. The brewery tap room offers tastings of their full range, from classic pale ales to seasonal specialties. The Gisborne Gold lager and Lighthouse IPA are local favourites. The brewery has been operating since 1989, making it one of New Zealand's older craft breweries. The tap room has a relaxed, social atmosphere and often hosts live music. It's a good place to meet locals and absorb Gisborne's friendly, unpretentious vibe. Tours of the brewing operation are available by arrangement.

reviews.summary: Good local craft beer in a friendly setting. Gisborne Gold is a crowd-pleaser. Great way to meet locals.

pois.name: Mahia Peninsula
pois.short: Remote peninsula with deserted beaches, excellent surf, and Rocket Lab's launch site nearby.
pois.commentary: The Mahia Peninsula is a wild, remote promontory jutting into the Pacific at the southern end of the Tairawhiti region. Long, deserted beaches offer excellent surf breaks and safe swimming. The peninsula has a strong Māori community and cultural significance. The landscape is dramatic — steep green hills dropping to golden beaches with views across Hawke Bay. Opoutama Beach is stunning. Nearby, Rocket Lab's launch facility at Mahia has brought unexpected international attention to the area, though the launch site itself is not publicly accessible. The peninsula retains a wonderfully untouristed character — this is genuine rural coastal New Zealand at its most authentic.

reviews.summary: Beautifully remote with stunning beaches. The untouristed character is refreshing. A genuine hidden gem.

pois.name: Gisborne Street Art & Murals
pois.short: Growing collection of large-scale murals celebrating Gisborne's Māori and Pacific identity.
pois.commentary: Gisborne's growing collection of street art and large-scale murals reflects the city's strong Māori and Pacific identity. Murals on buildings throughout the CBD depict local legends, cultural figures, and the region's connection to the sea. The artwork ranges from photorealistic portraits to stylised Māori motifs and contemporary Pacific art. A self-guided walking route covers the main murals in about an hour. The art scene connects to Gisborne's broader creative community, which draws energy from the region's cultural richness and the city's surf-and-sun lifestyle. The murals are constantly being added to, making each visit potentially different from the last.

reviews.summary: Growing street art scene with cultural depth. The Māori and Pacific themes are powerful. Easy self-guided walk.

pois.name: Mount Hikurangi / Hikurangi Maunga
pois.short: Sacred mountain of Ngāti Porou with giant whakairo carvings at the summit.
pois.commentary: Mount Hikurangi (1,752m) is the sacred mountain of the Ngāti Porou people and holds deep spiritual significance in Māori tradition — it is said to be the first land to emerge when Māui fished up the North Island. At the summit, nine magnificent whakairo (carved figures) represent characters from Māori creation narratives. Access requires a permit from Ngāti Porou and is managed through guided tours or self-guided permits with a 4WD vehicle. The sunrise from the summit is the first on mainland New Zealand. The mountain's spiritual significance means visitors must treat the experience with deep respect. Weather can change rapidly at altitude. A profoundly meaningful cultural and natural experience.

reviews.summary: A deeply spiritual and physically rewarding experience. The summit carvings are powerful, and the sunrise is unforgettable. Visitors appreciate the cultural significance.

pois.name: Makorori Beach
pois.short: World-class surf beach north of Gisborne with consistent waves.
pois.commentary: Makorori Beach is one of New Zealand's premier surf breaks, located just 10 minutes north of Gisborne city. The beach offers consistent, high-quality waves that attract surfers from around the country, with both beach breaks and a reliable right-hand point break at the northern end. The beach faces east, picking up Pacific swells year-round, and the offshore morning winds create clean conditions. It's also a beautiful swimming beach on calm days, with golden sand backed by native bush. The Makorori Walkway connects to neighbouring Wainui Beach, creating a stunning coastal walk. Surf lessons are available from Gisborne-based operators. The beach hosts national surfing competitions annually.

reviews.summary: Surfers rave about the consistent waves and uncrowded conditions. The beach is beautiful and the coastal walkway adds value. A gem of the East Cape.

pois.name: Gisborne Astro Tour
pois.short: The East Coast's only astro tour — a two-hour guided exploration of the night sky in one of New Zealand's darkest regions.
pois.commentary: Gisborne Astro Tour takes advantage of the East Cape's minimal light pollution to offer captivating two-hour explorations of the southern sky. Your guide uses powerful telescopes and laser pointers to reveal planets, star clusters, nebulae and the Milky Way in extraordinary detail, weaving in both Western astronomy and Māori star lore (tātai arorangi). The experience is weather-dependent and runs on clear nights — booking ahead is essential as group sizes are deliberately small. For anyone interested in the night sky, this is one of the most accessible and well-run stargazing experiences in the North Island.

reviews.summary: Guides are passionate and knowledgeable. The dark skies here are exceptional. The integration of Māori star lore adds depth. Weather cancellations are the only downside.

pois.name: Te Kuri Farm Walkway
pois.short: A coastal farm walkway with panoramic views over Poverty Bay, crossing private farmland between the coast and bush-clad hills.
pois.commentary: Te Kuri Farm Walkway crosses a working sheep and cattle station on the headlands above Poverty Bay, offering views that stretch from Young Nick's Head to Mahia Peninsula. The track climbs through pastoral land to ridgeline viewpoints before dropping through pockets of native bush to the coast. It's one of those generous New Zealand arrangements where a farmer opens their land to walkers — respect the privilege by following all signage, closing gates, and avoiding lambing season. The sense of space and solitude here is extraordinary.

reviews.summary: The views are spectacular and the walk is peaceful. Access requires permission — check locally. Some sections are steep. A genuine off-the-beaten-track experience.

pois.name: Te Araroa Glow Worm Caves
pois.short: Secret glow-worm caves near Te Araroa on the East Cape — intimate and uncrowded compared to Waitomo.
pois.commentary: The glow-worm caves near Te Araroa are one of the East Cape's best-kept secrets. Local guides lead small groups into limestone caves where thousands of glow-worms (Arachnocampa luminosa) create their own constellations in the darkness. Unlike the commercialised Waitomo experience, these tours are intimate, unhurried, and run by people with deep connections to the land. The caves are on private land and accessible only with a guide — enquire at Te Araroa. The experience is magical and all the more special for being so far from the tourist trail.

reviews.summary: An intimate and magical experience far from the crowds. Local guides are warm and knowledgeable. Availability is limited — book well ahead. Bring warm clothing for the caves.

pois.name: Waiapu Valley Heritage Drive
pois.short: A drive through the Waiapu Valley past ornate carved churches, marae, and the heartland of Ngāti Porou culture.
pois.commentary: The Waiapu Valley road inland from Ruatoria follows the Waiapu River through the heartland of Ngāti Porou, one of the largest iwi in Aotearoa. The valley is dotted with intricately carved churches — each one a masterwork of Māori art fused with Christian architecture — and marae that are the living centres of community life. This is the rohe (territory) of Sir Apirana Ngata, whose vision of cultural renaissance shaped modern New Zealand. Drive slowly, respect the communities you pass through, and stop at Tikitiki's St Mary's Church if you see only one carved church on the East Cape.

reviews.summary: A deeply moving cultural drive. The carved churches are extraordinary. Visitors should be respectful of the communities. Some roads are gravel and narrow. Best combined with East Cape road trip.

pois.name: Lottin Point / Wakatiri
pois.short: A remote headland on the East Cape with pristine beaches, excellent fishing, and a genuine end-of-the-road atmosphere.
pois.commentary: Lottin Point (Wakatiri) juts into the Pacific at one of the most remote stretches of the East Cape, where the road narrows to a track and the beaches are yours alone. The rocky foreshore offers some of the best shore fishing on the coast — snapper, kahawai and kingfish are all possibilities — while the beaches are pristine crescents of golden sand. Basic camping is available but facilities are minimal; bring everything you need. The night sky here is extraordinary, with zero light pollution. This is New Zealand at its most untouched.

reviews.summary: The remoteness is the main attraction. Fishing is excellent. Facilities are very basic — self-sufficient campers only. The road in can be rough. An unforgettable escape.

pois.name: Matawai Historic Timber & Rail Heritage
pois.short: A tiny inland settlement preserving the memory of the East Cape's once-mighty timber industry and bush tramway network.
pois.commentary: Matawai sits in the bush-clad ranges between Gisborne and Ōpōtiki, at the heart of what was once a vast timber-milling district. The settlement's heritage includes remnants of the bush tramways that hauled native timber from the ranges, a historic school, and stories of the isolated communities that lived and worked in these forests. Today it's a peaceful spot on SH2 with a general store, a few houses, and an atmosphere of quiet persistence. Stop, stretch your legs, and reflect on the extraordinary labour that built these communities in some of the most rugged country in the North Island.

reviews.summary: A brief but interesting heritage stop. The setting is atmospheric. Very little remains physically but the story is compelling. Best combined with the scenic drive over the ranges.

pois.name: Cook's Cove & Hole in the Wall Walk
pois.short: Walk through farmland and bush to a secluded cove with a natural rock arch — one of the East Coast's most scenic short walks.
pois.commentary: The Cook's Cove Walkway starts near Tolaga Bay and crosses private farmland before dropping through native bush to a sheltered cove where Captain Cook anchored in 1769. The highlight is the dramatic 'Hole in the Wall' — a natural sea arch carved through a rocky headland, visible from a clifftop lookout. The walk takes about 2.5 hours return and offers coastal views along the way. The cove itself is pristine and sheltered, perfect for swimming if conditions allow. The combination of pastoral landscape, native bush, dramatic geology and maritime history makes this one of the most rewarding short walks on the East Coast.

reviews.summary: A beautiful walk with a dramatic payoff. The Hole in the Wall is impressive. The farmland sections are pleasant. Allow time to explore the cove. Sturdy footwear recommended.

pois.name: Gisborne Chardonnay Wine Trail
pois.short: Explore New Zealand's 'Chardonnay Capital' — a circuit of boutique wineries producing exceptional cool-climate wines on the East Cape.
pois.commentary: Gisborne (Tairāwhiti) is New Zealand's third-largest wine region and holds the unofficial title of 'Chardonnay Capital of New Zealand.' The warm climate and alluvial soils produce rich, complex chardonnays alongside excellent gewürztraminer, viognier, and other white varietals. A self-drive cellar-door circuit connects wineries including Bushmere Estate, Matawhero (site of the first commercial chardonnay planting in NZ), Wright's Vineyard, and several boutique producers. The cellar doors are intimate and unhurried — you're likely to be tasting with the winemaker. The region remains genuinely underrated, which means excellent wines without the crowds of Marlborough or Hawke's Bay.

reviews.summary: Excellent chardonnay in an uncrowded, authentic setting. The winemakers are welcoming and accessible. The region is underrated. Great value compared to more famous wine regions. Self-drive is best.

pois.name: Te Waha o Rerekohu — Ancient Pōhutukawa
pois.short: New Zealand's oldest pōhutukawa tree, over 600 years old, at Te Araroa.
pois.commentary: Te Waha o Rerekohu is believed to be New Zealand's oldest and largest pōhutukawa, estimated at over 600 years old with a trunk circumference of nearly 20 metres. This taonga (treasure) holds deep significance for local iwi and stands as a living link to the land's ancient history.

reviews.summary: Awe-inspiring ancient tree — a living treasure.

pois.name: Cooks Cove & Hole in the Wall
pois.short: Coastal walkway to a dramatic natural rock arch overlooking a secluded cove.
pois.commentary: The Cooks Cove Walkway crosses private farmland and native bush to reach a secluded cove where Captain Cook once anchored. The highlight is the Hole in the Wall — a dramatic natural arch in the cliff face offering framed views of Cooks Cove below.

reviews.summary: Beautiful walk to an incredible natural arch — don't miss it.

pois.name: East Cape Lighthouse Walk
pois.short: 700-step climb to mainland New Zealand's easternmost lighthouse.
pois.commentary: A climb of approximately 700 steps leads to the East Cape Lighthouse, the easternmost point on mainland New Zealand. The panoramic views from the top span the Pacific Ocean in every direction, and it's a popular spot for catching the first sunrise of the new year.

reviews.summary: Worth every step — stunning ocean panorama at the top.

pois.name: Waikanae Creek Boardwalk
pois.short: Waterfront boardwalk along Gisborne's Waikanae Creek estuary.
pois.commentary: This pleasant boardwalk follows Waikanae Creek from the city centre to the beach, passing through the estuary where white-faced herons, pied stilts, and kingfishers are commonly spotted. A lovely way to connect the town with the waterfront on foot.

reviews.summary: Nice easy walk connecting town to beach with good birdwatching.

pois.name: Te Araroa Village Heritage Walk
pois.short: Heritage walk through the remote East Cape village at the end of SH35.
pois.commentary: Te Araroa is one of New Zealand's most remote settlements, the final village before the East Cape. A heritage walk passes the historic school, church, and general store, plus the ancient pōhutukawa tree — a glimpse into life at the far eastern edge of the country.

reviews.summary: Charmingly remote — a real end-of-the-road feeling.

pois.name: Gray's Bush Scenic Reserve
pois.short: Rare remnant of original lowland bush with a gentle walking loop.
pois.commentary: Gray's Bush is a precious remnant of the original podocarp-broadleaf forest that once covered the Gisborne Plains. This small reserve protects ancient tōtara, matai, and kahikatea trees with a gentle loop walk through cathedral-like stands — a botanical time capsule.

reviews.summary: Precious forest remnant — the ancient trees are impressive.

pois.name: Hackfalls Arboretum
pois.short: A remote Tiniroto farm arboretum with rare trees, lakeside planting and a remarkable botanical collection.
pois.commentary: Hackfalls Arboretum is one of those Tairāwhiti places that rewards people who enjoy the road as much as the destination. Near Tiniroto, between Gisborne and Wairoa, the Berry family farm became a major arboretum, known especially for its tree collections spread through pasture and around lakes. The visit is seasonal and opening details should be checked before travelling, but the idea is simple and lovely: a working rural landscape used as a living tree library. Autumn colour, unusual oaks, maples and specimen trees make it a very different East Coast experience from beaches and headlands.

reviews.summary: Garden and tree enthusiasts value the rare collection, remote setting and autumn colour; planning ahead for access is essential.

pois.name: Dive Tatapouri Reef Ecology Tour
pois.short: A guided reef ecology experience north of Gisborne, focused on rays, tides and marine respect.
pois.commentary: Tatapouri’s reef tour turns a shallow coastal platform into a living classroom. Guides read the tide, introduce rays and other marine life, and keep the emphasis on respect rather than spectacle, which is exactly what close wildlife encounters need.

pois.short: A local bush reserve north of Gisborne with shaded tracks and a small waterfall walk.
pois.commentary: Waihirere Domain is the sort of local green place visitors can easily miss. The tracks offer shade, birdsong and a waterfall without requiring a long drive, making it a useful reset between coast, town and wine-country stops.

pois.commentary: The Motu Trails can be rugged and remote, but the dunes section keeps things open and friendly. It follows the coast with enough curve and breeze to feel like an outing, while hinting at the wilder backcountry riding further inland.

pois.commentary: Ruatoria is not a conventional attraction, but the landscape around it is essential Tairāwhiti. Mount Hikurangi holds the horizon with cultural significance and physical force, reminding travellers that the East Coast is lived-in country, not just a scenic route.

pois.short: A remnant forest reserve on Mahia Peninsula, adding a green inland pause to a beach-and-surf coast.
pois.commentary: Mahia Peninsula is often imagined through beaches, headlands and launch-day horizons, but the scenic reserve offers a quieter inland layer. The bush gives shade and texture to a peninsula better known for sea light, making the place feel more whole.

pois.name: Pouawa Beach
pois.short: A seasonal camping and swimming beach north of Gisborne, with fishing, diving and open-coast light.
pois.commentary: Pouawa Beach is a practical coastal pause north of Gisborne: simple, open and best approached with respect for seasonal camping rules. It suits travellers who want a Tairawhiti beach that still feels local, with surf, fishing and wide Pacific light doing the talking.

pois.short: A named peak that helps fill the inland skyline of Tairāwhiti.
pois.commentary: Maungahaumi / Maungahaumia helps mark the high country and skyline around Tairāwhiti. Peaks are not all visitor-ready tracks, so the value here is orientation first: a named point that explains what you are seeing from roads, lookouts and nearby walks.
